posts.name: Barry Rothof
posts.cooked:
• 2011 Arnaud Ente Puligny-Montrachet 1er Cru Les Referts - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Puligny-Montrachet 1er Cru (10/9/2016)

Youthful colour. Incredible nose! Easily mistaken for a d’Auvenay, chalky, flinty, stoney, wonderfully mineral, juicy and powerful long. Awesome wine! Arnaud Ente is fast becoming the next white Burgundy cult star and hard to find! (94 pts.)

Posted from CellarTracker

posts.name: Мајкл Ристев
posts.cooked: this is quite possibly my favorite white burgundy being made at the moment. the 2007 is a stunner.

posts.name: Carlos Delpin
posts.cooked: His Meursault and Volnay are superb too.

posts.name: Andy Steinman
posts.cooked: Happy to have some of these in the Cellar!

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked: Stunning wines, very Coche-like. The Aligote is off the charts too.

posts.name: Michel Linden
posts.cooked:
m. ristev:

this is quite possibly my favorite white burgundy being made at the moment. the 2007 is a stunner.

snap
we had the 2007 a month ago - brilliant deft wine…
I want to give him some Chevalier Montrachet to play with

posts.name: Мајкл Ристев
posts.cooked: or any grand cru for that matter…

posts.name: Martin Steinley
posts.cooked: I couldn’t agree more with the assessment in this thread of Arnaud Ente’s wines. It was the 2007 version of this wine that really got my attention. I have worked my way through all but a couple of bottles of a case and all have been riveting. Sadly, the wholesale price of recent vintages is about five times the 2007.

posts.name: Мајкл Ристев
posts.cooked: i am very happy to still have a mixed case of his 07s left including 3 of the les referts. the interesting thing is that these wines have always been exceptionally expensive in france.

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked: Am I alone in thinking his wines are almost “too much”? I do enjoy them, but am happier drinking somewhat more restrained wines. If the prices weren’t so high, maybe there would be room in my cellar for Arnaud Ente’s wines, but as things are I don’t think about it much. I did pick up a bottle of 2014 Chassagne made by Benoit Ente on my last trip to Burgundy, but haven’t tried it yet. Any idea how the styles compare?

posts.name: Мајкл Ристев
posts.cooked: how so? his top wines rarely reach even 12.5%. there was a big change in style and vinification in the early 2000s.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
m. ristev:

how so? his top wines rarely reach even 12.5%. there was a big change in style and vinification in the early 2000s.

Seconded.

Apparently his wines are commercialized by a gérant in the Beaujolais who is responsible for pricing, including ex-domaine. That, and scarcity, are the only real downsides to Ente that I can see.

posts.name: Daniel Bailey
posts.cooked: I’ve loved all the Ente wines I’ve tried but pricing is really punitive.

posts.name: Peter Chiu
posts.cooked:
Alan Rath:

Am I alone in thinking his wines are almost “too much”? I do enjoy them, but am happier drinking somewhat more restrained wines. If the prices weren’t so high, maybe there would be room in my cellar for Arnaud Ente’s wines, but as things are I don’t think about it much. I did pick up a bottle of 2014 Chassagne made by Benoit Ente on my last trip to Burgundy, but haven’t tried it yet. Any idea how the styles compare?

No you are not alone. One of my friend said *too much (= oak bomb) * and the other loved it…

am happier drinking somewhat more restrained

Yes and I agree… …

There is a big difference in price between Benoit and Arnaud.

posts.name: Мајкл Ристев
posts.cooked: arnaud uses a maximum of 20% new oak

posts.name: Peter Chiu
posts.cooked:
m. ristev:

arnaud uses a maximum of 20% new oak

I told my friend about that but he did not finished his glass of…

posts.name: Martin Steinley
posts.cooked:
Peter Chiu:

There is a big difference in price between Benoit and Arnaud.

Akin to comparing Prudhon and Lamy in St.-Aubin. The former makes good, well-priced wines, while the latter makes head-spinning, now-expensive wines.
